abstract: This article examines the specialized terminological and conceptual framework employed by Muhammad Said Ramadan al-Buti in his seminal work, *al-Salafiyyah*. Particular attention is given to the concepts of "Arabian scales," the "scientific and ideological shield," and the "compass." Al-Buti defines these as the pillars of the methodological path (*manhaj*) established by the righteous predecessors (*al-salaf al-salih*), rooted in sincere faith and the precision of classical Arabic. By systematizing evidence from across the text, this research characterizes the divergent paths of scholars who adhere to these "scales of sanity" versus those who have strayed, evaluating the ideological consequences of both. Furthermore, the paper investigates al-Buti’s stance on the limits of inquiry. It contrasts his perspective with the rigid prohibitions against questioning favored by ultra-conservatives, as well as the unchecked intellectual license advocated by religious liberals. Through an analysis of al-Buti’s classification of knowledge and his nuanced linguistic style, the author demonstrates how this methodology harmonizes extreme viewpoints to propose a path of moderation in Islamic jurisprudence and thought.
